title.emphasis: Part III: Navigating the Labyrinth of California Employment Law

section.para: For any venture commissioning creative work from individuals in California, a deep and nuanced understanding of the state's uniquely stringent employment laws is not merely a matter of compliance—it is a matter of existential risk. Misclassifying an employee as an independent contractor can trigger devastating financial penalties, including back pay, unpaid overtime, meal and rest period violations, unpaid payroll taxes, and potential criminal liability for failure to maintain workers' compensation insurance.13 The legal framework established by Assembly Bill 5 (AB5) creates a formidable set of hurdles for any entity seeking to engage individual artists on a contract basis.

title.emphasis: Section 3.1: The Presumption of Employment: AB5 and the ABC Test

section.para: Effective since January 1, 2020, AB5 codified the California Supreme Court's landmark decision in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court and established the "ABC test" as the default standard for determining worker classification for most purposes under the California Labor Code and Unemployment Insurance Code.14 The foundational principle of the ABC test is a legal presumption that a worker is an employee. The burden of proof falls entirely on the hiring entity to demonstrate that the worker is an independent contractor by satisfying
all three of the following stringent conditions 14:

listitem.para: (A) The Control Prong: The worker must be free from the control and direction of the hiring entity in connection with the performance of the work, both under the terms of the contract and in actual practice. This prong examines whether the hiring entity retains the right to control the manner and means by which the work is accomplished, not just the final result. Direct supervision, setting work hours, or requiring the use of specific tools or processes would likely cause a hiring entity to fail this prong.14

(B) The Course of Business Prong: The worker must perform work that is outside the usual course of the hiring entity's business. This is often the most difficult prong for creative ventures to satisfy. If a company's primary business is, for example, developing and publishing video games, a graphic artist hired to create character art for a game is performing work that is central and integral to the company's core business. Similarly, for a venture whose mission is to "cultivate artistic talent and commission art," the act of commissioning an artist to create art is arguably directly within the usual course of its business, presenting a significant legal challenge under this prong.16

(C) The Independent Trade Prong: The worker must be customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, or business of the same nature as the work being performed. The hiring entity must prove that the worker has an existing independent business (e.g., they have their own LLC, market their services to other clients, have their own business cards and website) and that their status as an independent contractor is not dependent on the single engagement in question. A hiring entity cannot unilaterally create this status by requiring the worker to sign an independent contractor agreement or form an LLC as a condition of engagement.14

section.para: Failure to satisfy even one of these three prongs results in the worker being classified as an employee by default. This rigid, all-or-nothing structure makes the ABC test far more restrictive than the more flexible, multi-factor tests used in many other jurisdictions and under federal law.

title.emphasis: Section 3.2: The "Fine Artist" Exemption and the Borello Test

section.para: While the ABC test is the default, AB5 and its 2020 successor, AB 2257, carved out a series of exemptions for specific professions and contracting relationships. For the creative industries, the most significant of these is the "professional services" exemption, which can apply to individuals working as "fine artists".13

It is critical to understand that this is not an automatic exemption that classifies all fine artists as independent contractors. Rather, if the conditions for the exemption are met, it changes the legal test used for classification. Instead of the stringent ABC test, the relationship is evaluated under the older, more flexible multi-factor standard established in S. G. Borello & Sons, Inc. v. Department of Industrial Relations.14

To qualify for the "fine artist" exemption and have the Borello test apply, the hiring entity must demonstrate that the artist maintains a separate business location, has a business license, markets their services to the public, and negotiates their own rates, among other criteria.13

Once these threshold requirements are met, the Borello test is applied. Unlike the ABC test, Borello is a "totality of the circumstances" analysis that balances numerous factors, with no single factor being determinative. The most important factor—the "principal test"—is whether the hiring entity has the right to control the manner and means of accomplishing the desired result.14 Other factors weighed by the court include 13:

listitem.para: Whether the worker is engaged in a distinct occupation or business.
Whether the work is typically done under supervision or by a specialist without supervision.
The skill required for the occupation.
Who supplies the instrumentalities, tools, and place of work.
The length of time for which the services are to be performed.
The method of payment (by time or by the job).
Whether the work is a part of the regular business of the principal.
The parties' belief about the nature of the relationship (employee or contractor).

section.para: The existence of the "fine artist" exemption is a crucial strategic consideration. It provides a potential pathway to legitimately classify artists as independent contractors, but it requires a different and more fact-intensive legal strategy. The venture must not only ensure the artist meets the preconditions for the exemption but also structure the working relationship and the SOW to align with the Borello factors, emphasizing the artist's autonomy, independent business status, and control over their creative process.

title.emphasis: Section 3.3: Administrative Thresholds and the Definition of a "Substantial" Contract

section.para: A key operational strategy is to classify contracts based on the administrative burden they trigger, allowing for streamlined processes for smaller engagements while applying rigorous compliance measures where legally required. This creates a practical distinction between "insubstantial" and "substantial" agreements.

listitem.para: Financial Threshold: The most common trigger for a contract becoming "substantial" is when payments to an independent contractor meet or exceed the federal reporting threshold, compelling the hiring entity to issue an IRS Form 1099-NEC (Nonemployee Compensation).7

For the 2025 Tax Year: The reporting threshold remains at $600. If a venture pays an individual independent contractor a total of $600 or more in a calendar year, it must file a Form 1099-NEC with the IRS and provide a copy to the contractor.7

Upcoming Change for 2026 and Beyond: A significant change is imminent. The "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" increases this reporting threshold from $600 to $2,000. This increase applies to payments made after December 31, 2025, meaning it will take effect for the 2026 tax year, with forms filed in early 2027.17 This new $2,000 threshold will also be indexed for inflation for calendar years beginning after 2026.17 This change will significantly reduce the administrative burden for smaller, one-off projects in the future.

Legal Status Trigger: The most significant trigger, however, is not financial but legal. A contract automatically becomes "substantial" at any dollar amount if the relationship is determined to be one of employer-employee.7 This classification immediately triggers the highest level of administrative overhead, including the requirement to issue an IRS Form W-2, withhold federal and state payroll taxes (including Social Security and Medicare), pay unemployment insurance taxes, and provide workers' compensation insurance coverage.7 The administrative and financial costs associated with an employment relationship far exceed those of an independent contractor relationship, making correct classification paramount.

section.para: By using these triggers, the venture can create a tiered administrative system. "Insubstantial" contracts (those below the 1099-NEC threshold that do not create an employment relationship) can be managed with less overhead, while "substantial" contracts automatically trigger rigorous accounting, reporting, and compliance protocols.

title.emphasis: Table 2: Independent Contractor Classification: Federal vs. California

section.para: The legal landscape for worker classification varies significantly between federal and California state law. A relationship that might be permissible under the federal standard could be deemed an employment relationship in California. This table illustrates the critical differences between the tests.

row.entry: **Factor / Test**
**Federal FLSA "Economic Reality" Test**
**California "ABC" Test (Default)**
**California "*****Borello*****" Test (For Exemptions)**
**Core Principle**
A "totality of the circumstances" test to determine if the worker is **economically dependent** on the employer or in business for themselves.20
A rigid, **three-pronged statutory test**. The hiring entity must prove **all three** prongs are met to rebut the presumption of employment.14
A multi-factor, "totality of the circumstances" balancing test. The primary factor is the **right to control** the manner and means of work.13
**Control**
**Factor 4: Nature and Degree of Control.** One of six factors to be balanced. Considers scheduling, supervision, and ability to work for others.20
**Prong A: Free from Control.** A mandatory requirement. The worker must be free from control both in contract and in fact.14
**Primary Factor.** The most important consideration in the balancing test is the hiring entity's right to control.13
**Course of Business**
**Factor 5: Integral Part of Business.** One of six factors. Considers if the work is critical or central to the employer's business.20
**Prong B: Outside Usual Course of Business.** A mandatory requirement. Often the most difficult prong for creative ventures to meet.14
**One of several secondary factors.** Weighed as part of the overall analysis, but not a dispositive requirement.13
**Independent Business**
**Factor 1, 2, & 6.** Assessed through factors like opportunity for profit/loss, worker's investment, and use of skill/initiative for business growth.20
**Prong C: Customarily Engaged in Independent Trade.** A mandatory requirement. The worker must have an existing, independent business.14
**One of several secondary factors.** Considers if the worker is engaged in a distinct occupation or business.13
**Flexibility**
**High.** No single factor is determinative. The overall economic reality of the relationship is considered.21
**None.** Failure to prove any single prong results in employee classification.14
**Moderate.** A balancing test where factors are weighed against each other. More favorable to finding contractor status than the ABC test.13
**Implication**
Businesses must analyze the entire relationship. More permissive than California's ABC test.
Extremely restrictive. Poses a high risk for businesses that rely on contractors performing core functions.
The preferred test for businesses engaging exempt professionals like "fine artists," but requires careful structuring of the relationship to satisfy the factors.
